video_title: A VC's Playbook For Investing in the AI Bubble
gist: Notable Capital partner Jeff Richards lays out a VC's framework for investing through the AI 'bubble': stay in the 'token path' (businesses that improve as models improve), favor Google/Meta/cybersecurity as durable AI plays, expect a wave of hyper-efficient, fast-growing private companies (Anthropic, Databricks, SpaceX, OpenAI) to IPO and flood the LP ecosystem with liquidity, and treat sharp single-day 20-30% melt-ups in real tech names as a genuine late-cycle warning sign even as he believes the secular AI demand curve still slopes up for a decade.

themes.theme: Token-path framework for AI software winners

insights.claim: Jeff Richards' core stock-picking screen for the AI era is whether a company is 'in the token path'—i.e., its business mechanically improves as underlying models improve and token consumption rises; companies not in the token path won't be raising guidance and should be avoided.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: A reusable screen: track which software names are taking up full-year guidance as model quality jumps—those are 'token path' beneficiaries; laggards on guidance are structurally exposed.
insights.quote: If you're in the token path, meaning as the models get better, your business gets better. Anybody who's in the token path saw their business get better

insights.claim: Richards argues the quant/ETF market wrongly lumped CrowdStrike, Snowflake, Datadog and Palo Alto Networks in with commoditized 'SaaS,' and that mispricing created the opportunity: those four stocks rallied 70-80% off their Q1 lows after the AI-displacement fear faded.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Indiscriminate 'SaaS apocalypse' selloffs of differentiated, AI-leveraged software create buyable dislocations; the names that snapped back hardest signal where the durable franchises are.
insights.quote: If the quant algos and ETFs have convinced you that uh CrowdStrike and Snowflake are the same as SaaS based software, that I don't know what to do for you because they're very different business models

insights.claim: Richards does not expect a near-term collapse in legacy SaaS revenue—Salesforce, ServiceNow and Workday businesses are durable for the next 12-24 months—but the open question is whether they can get into the token path over 2-3 years, and many will have to do it via M&A because they lack the internal teams to build AI-native products.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Legacy SaaS is a 'durable but not dead' trade near-term; the multi-year tail risk is real, making M&A optionality (acquiring AI-native teams) the key thing to watch.
insights.quote: I don't believe you're going to see a huge drop off in the next 12 to 24 months. It's just those businesses are durable, they have great products, they're now starting to harness AI.

insights.claim: Anthropic is now at a ~$47 billion revenue run rate, up from $9 billion at the end of last year—and Richards notes Anthropic added more new run-rate revenue in Q1 alone than the entire IGV software index added in all of 2025 ($60B total, $30B of which was Microsoft Azure).
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: A historically unprecedented growth rate that reframes how to value private AI labs and explains the positive derivative effects flowing to memory, GPUs and infra.
insights.quote: Anthropic exceeded that in Q1. So this is something we've really never seen before

insights.claim: Over 50% of Anthropic's AI calls are for coding, while enterprise functions like finance, accounting and marketing are still sub-10% adoption—implying the largest waves of token consumption (and the associated spend scrutiny) are still ahead.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Coding is the proven first vertical; finance/accounting/marketing represent the next legs of demand growth—watch tools that measure non-coding AI output, a coming budget battleground.
insights.quote: Over 50% of the AI calls for for Anthropic, at least, are are for coding... those other functions... We're still like sub 10%.

insights.claim: Richards flags the recurring 'SanDisk and Micron' memory beneficiaries plus Nvidia and AMD as the second-order trade off Anthropic-style token growth, because surging inference demand makes memory and compute supply the binding constraint.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Frames memory (Micron, SanDisk) as a derivative play on AI-lab token growth, not just GPUs—useful for building a basket around the constrained supply chain.

themes.theme: Cybersecurity as the obvious durable AI trade

insights.claim: Richards calls cybersecurity the most obvious durable AI growth area because agents acting with broad access to corporate systems massively expand the attack surface, and the number one thing holding back enterprise AI adoption is security—he expects CrowdStrike and Palo Alto growth rates to potentially accelerate like Palantir's did.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Positions cybersecurity TAM as underestimated and a gating factor for all enterprise AI; watch for growth-rate acceleration at CRWD/PANW as the bull catalyst.
insights.quote: the number one thing in my opinion that is holding back large corporations from adopting AI en masse is cybersecurity

insights.claim: Richards highlights CrowdStrike's move from ~$370-400 to ~$760 in roughly two months (orderly ramp from mid-April, then parabolic in the last week) as an example of a fundamentally-driven name where the price action got ahead of itself, but where he won't trade around it because he isn't a trader.
insights.speaker: host (Dan Nathan) and Jeff Richards
insights.so_what: A concrete tape: CRWD nearly doubled in ~2 months; the parabolic final week is the kind of melt-up that flags crowding even in a high-quality name.

insights.claim: On current numbers Richards cites the software comp set as DataDog growing 24% at 18x forward revenue, Cloudflare 29% at 28x, CrowdStrike 23% at 30x, and Palantir the outlier growing 63% at 43x—noting anything above ~10x forward revenue is already an aggressive valuation, with the bull case resting on growth-rate acceleration.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Gives an explicit valuation map for the AI-software complex; the multiples only work if growth accelerates, so any deceleration is the asymmetric downside trigger.
insights.quote: you would argue anything above maybe 10 times is an aggressive valuation. But I think what folks are probably thinking is that those growth rates are going to accelerate.

themes.theme: Google as the best pure-play AI bet

insights.claim: Richards calls Google probably the best pure-play AI bet ownable because it is vertically integrated across the whole stack (TPUs, GCP infrastructure, AI mode in search, YouTube, Android, advertising, travel) with 12-13 platforms over a billion users each—'if you want QQQ, you want a lot of Google.'
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Frames GOOGL as the lowest-controversy way to own the full AI stack; vertical integration (especially TPUs) is the differentiator versus single-layer bets.
insights.quote: I would argue Google is probably the best pure play bet on AI you can own because of all the different points of differentiation you mentioned.

insights.claim: Richards reads Berkshire Hathaway's $10 billion direct purchase in Google's ~$80 billion equity raise (at roughly double the price Google traded a year ago) as a notably bullish signal—one of the world's most conservative investors making what is largely an AI-infrastructure bet, despite Berkshire's history of missing internet, mobile and social.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: A high-signal endorsement of AI infra from a value investor; the $10B Berkshire stake validates Google's infrastructure thesis at a full valuation.
insights.quote: one of the arguably most conservative investors in the world just put $10 billion into Google at today's valuation

insights.claim: Richards reframes Google's equity raise as a smart capital-allocation move rather than a red flag: with the stock at the highest valuation it has ever been (~26x forward), issuing equity minimizes dilution, and he argues you'd rather see a company sell stock at a high valuation than buy back stock at a high valuation—several large software firms recently announced big buybacks then fell 30%.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Contrarian capital-allocation lens: equity issuance at peak multiples is a positive, buybacks at peak multiples a negative—useful for judging any AI mega-cap's financing choices, and a possible emerging trend.
insights.quote: you'd rather have them selling stock at a high valuation than buying stock at a high valuation

insights.claim: Meta is the only stock Richards personally bought in the last 30 days because he views it as undervalued—a founder-led company whose VR/infrastructure bet is now poised to pay off in AI, with consumer-business upside (better targeting, algorithms, monetization) that the market hasn't yet connected to its CapEx spend.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: His single highest-conviction recent buy; the catalyst is the market re-rating Meta once CapEx-to-monetization leverage becomes visible, analogous to Google ~18 months ago.
insights.quote: Meta is the only thing I bought in the last 30 days. I just think it's undervalued.

insights.claim: Richards suggests Meta could convert its AI infrastructure into a third-party compute platform—citing Elon Musk's xAI deal to pay Anthropic ~$1.25 billion a month (with a 90-day out on both sides)—becoming a provider to Anthropic, OpenAI or Mistral, an optionality the market isn't pricing.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Hidden optionality: Meta's CapEx could become a revenue-generating compute platform; the xAI/Anthropic $1.25B/mo deal is the template and a data point on compute pricing.

insights.claim: Richards rates Microsoft a TBD outside Azure: Copilot is widely seen as inferior to Claude, the company lacks a consumer-distribution edge like Apple/Google/Meta, and the fate of the Office/PowerPoint/Word franchise is the open question—he wouldn't be shocked to see Satya Nadella pursue a partnership or M&A this year despite regulatory constraints.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Frames MSFT as Azure-strong but app-franchise-uncertain; watch for a 2026 partnership or M&A catalyst, and Copilot's competitiveness versus Claude as the swing factor.
insights.quote: Copilot has been sort of their signature product, which I think most people would acknowledge is not nearly as good as something like Claude.

insights.claim: Richards's safest-AI-bet ranking is Google first, then Amazon and Meta; he flags Amazon and Meta as 'unloved' partly because the market can't yet connect CapEx to future benefit, and points to portfolio company Quince (e-commerce, led by partner Hans) where AI has transformed logistics, inventory, pricing and marketing as evidence the same leverage is coming at Amazon and Meta.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Ranks the mega-cap 'safe' AI plays and uses private-company operating data (Quince) as a leading indicator for Amazon/Meta consumer-AI leverage.
insights.quote: where are these safest quote-unquote plays in AI? It's Google, it's probably Amazon, it's probably Meta.

themes.theme: Enterprise AI adoption: spend, build-vs-buy and talent

insights.claim: Richards pushes back on the 'AI spend will go down' narrative: instead of cutting, enterprises will shift to rigorously measuring output—his own 40-person firm and portfolio companies (one doing 48-hour performance reviews) track productivity output rather than token consumption, and spend stays elevated.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Counters the bear thesis that consumption-based sticker shock cuts AI budgets; the real second-order play is the wave of tools measuring AI output/productivity.
insights.quote: Peo- people immediately jumped to, "Oh, the spending's going to go down." And I'm like, "Mm, guys, that's not what's going to happen. They're going to measure it

insights.claim: Richards reports a Fortune 50 company (>$100B revenue) only just signed its first Anthropic deal a month ago, evidence that even the largest enterprises are at 'day one' of AI adoption and that the corporate use case has years of runway.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: On-the-ground evidence that enterprise AI penetration is far earlier than the stock prices imply—supports a long demand runway for model providers and infra.
insights.quote: I said, "Hey, where are you guys in terms of deploying AI?" He said, "Oh, we just signed a deal with Anthropic."... So, I think we are still very much at day one

insights.claim: Richards anchors the AI-margin bull case in history: S&P 500 profit margins were flat for 20 years before 1995, then rose 7 points (from 7% to 14%) from 1995-2020 because of the internet—implying AI can drive a similar structural margin expansion, though the bridge for industrials, oil & gas and retail will take years.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: A long-horizon margin-expansion thesis that justifies CapEx; near-term the value accrues to integrators (Accenture, Deloitte) bridging the testing-to-production gap.
insights.quote: profit margins for the S&P 500 prior to 1995, they were flat for 20 years. And then from '95 to 2020, they went up seven points, from seven to 14. Why? Because of the internet.

insights.claim: Richards distinguishes where AI-native build-vs-buy disruption hits: sales/marketing, design and creative are easy to displace (Canva/Figma ate Adobe share), but back-office ERP, general ledger, payables/receivables are hard categories where large enterprises want a vendor to blame on risk/cybersecurity—so legacy SaaS is safest in the back office.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: A category-level disruption map: short/cautious on front-office creative incumbents, more defensive on back-office ERP—useful for picking which SaaS names are exposed.
insights.quote: large enterprises want somebody to blame if something goes wrong from a cybersecurity or a risk standpoint

insights.claim: Richards says private companies of 50-100 employees are already building internal AI operating systems and peeling off third-party SaaS products, but a Fortune 50 spending $3-4B/year on IT won't rip out and replace its stack anytime soon—so the race is whether incumbents build modern AI-native products before emerging players take them out.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Build-internally is a real threat at the small-company tier but slow at the enterprise tier; the timing gap determines whether SaaS incumbents survive or get disrupted.
insights.quote: The idea that a, you know, Fortune 50 company that spends 3 billion or 4 billion dollars a year on IT is going to suddenly rip out all of its existing platforms and replace them with native AI is probably not going to happen anytime soon.

insights.claim: Richards identifies talent as one of Silicon Valley's most underrated dynamics: large enterprise software companies literally cannot recruit top AI engineers—a 30/35-year-old will choose Anthropic, OpenAI, Vercel, Base10 or Fireworks over ServiceNow—and are losing existing staff, which will force acqui-hires (e.g., Zuckerberg's Scale team deal as a template).
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Talent flight is a structural headwind for legacy enterprise software and a driver of forced acqui-hire M&A—watch which incumbents pay up for AI teams.
insights.quote: if you are a really talented 35-year-old or 30-year-old AI engineer... you have a job an opportunity to go to Anthropic or ServiceNow, which one are you going to choose? ... You're going to go to Anthropic.

themes.theme: Semis, robotics and the bubble/supply-constraint debate

insights.claim: Richards notes a VC resurgence into semiconductors—a category VCs avoided for decades—citing Cerebras's IPO and an acquisition by Groq, but cautions that Cerebras took ~14 years to build, so semis are long-gestation bets.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Signals renewed VC appetite for semis after wins like Cerebras, but the 14-year build time tempers expectations for quick returns.

insights.claim: Richards says robotics is now the category attracting the most capital and attention at Notable and peer firms—not humanoid 'Optimus next to you at a football game,' but specialized use cases in manufacturing, logistics and transportation (Waymo cited as a successful 'robot'), framed as a 5-10 year innovation cycle.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Robotics is the next major VC capital-flow frontier; the near-term value is in specialized industrial/logistics robots (and AV like Waymo), not consumer humanoids.
insights.quote: the category that is attracting a lot of attention and a lot of capital right now is is robotics

insights.claim: Richards admits the firm and he personally avoid semis beyond owning QQQ, and frames the central semi question as whether the market is still underestimating GPU demand going forward—conceding everyone underestimated it the first time (or they'd have put their net worth in Nvidia, AMD, Broadcom a decade ago).
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Reframes the semi debate as a demand-estimation question; the bull case persists only if GPU demand is still being underestimated, which is the crux of the bubble call.
insights.quote: everybody underestimated how much demand there was for GPUs... So, the question you have to ask yourself is are we underestimating it going forward?

insights.claim: Richards' bubble test is whether the entire supply chain—from Anthropic down to GPU providers—is genuinely capacity-constrained, evidenced by users being rate-limited on Anthropic daily; demand exceeds capacity today, but he and Nathan agree the cycle will inevitably asymptote and flip to oversupply (they've 'been through seven cycles').
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards and host (Dan Nathan)
insights.so_what: Rate-limiting is the real-time tell that demand still exceeds supply; the inflection to watch is when supply finally exceeds demand—the classic cycle top.
insights.quote: everyday people are getting rate limited on Anthropic. And so if you believe that that is happening throughout the chain... will we will we will at some point out cuz it would have to asymptote

insights.claim: Nathan argues the market is failing to discount obvious risks—double/triple ordering of chips due to capacity constraints, plus the historical precedent that beloved names like Tesla, Nvidia, Netflix and Meta have each fallen ~70% from highs—and a 2022-style rate-driven correction (inflation not yet tamed) could hit CapEx and the whole complex within ~18 months.
insights.speaker: host (Dan Nathan)
insights.so_what: The bear scenario: pull-forward demand, double-ordering and a rate shock could trigger a sharp drawdown; nothing being discounted today is the risk-asymmetry the host flags.
insights.quote: we've seen stocks that have the ability to go down some of your most beloved stocks, whether it was Tesla or Nvidia or Netflix or meta whatever... they went down 70% from the highs

insights.claim: Richards anchors his long-term TAM bull case on the cloud analogy: in 2010 analysts pegged web-services TAM at $5-6B annually when AWS was a $500M business; today AWS, Azure and GCP generate ~$320B/year—so if the AI TAM estimate is even in the same 'zip code' of wrongness, demand is being vastly underestimated.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Quantifies the 'TAM is underestimated' thesis with a 60x cloud miss; the structural bull case for a decade of AI infra demand despite cyclical wobbles.
insights.quote: back in 2010, analysts estimated the TAM for web services was 5 to 6 billion dollars annually... Today, AWS, Microsoft, and GCP generate 320 billion dollars a year

insights.claim: Richards names data-center and power buildout permitting as the constraint that worries him most—comparing opposition to data centers to the opposition that blocked nuclear—warning that limited power and data-center capacity, alongside interest rates, geopolitics and US fiscal/tax pressure, are the real threats to the AI buildout.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Power and data-center permitting—not demand—is the binding real-world constraint; energy/power infrastructure and policy are the variables that could throttle the whole cycle.
insights.quote: how about just people constraining the amount of data center capacity we're building? To me, that is the craziest thing I've ever seen.

themes.theme: The coming IPO wave and private-company economics

insights.claim: Richards expects SpaceX, Anthropic and OpenAI to potentially IPO in the next ~6-12 months, and behind them a wave of companies with $500M-$2B+ revenue growing far faster than anything public—where the market won't know how to value growth north of 100% (Palantir's ~43x at 60% growth is the current ceiling) and will be surprised by their margins, cash flow and efficiency.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: A near-term IPO supercycle is coming; the valuation framework for >100% growth is undefined, creating both repricing risk and opportunity—watch how the first deals price.
insights.quote: there's a whole wave of companies that will hopefully go out behind them that are call it 500 million to a couple billion of revenue with growth rates that are way higher than anything we see in the public markets today

insights.claim: Richards says the new private-company operating model is radically more efficient: the old 'good' benchmark of ~$300K ARR per FTE (circa 2019-2021) is now the low end, with several Notable portfolio companies generating millions of dollars of ARR per FTE—'a very different operating model than your grandpa's software company.'
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: ARR-per-FTE has jumped roughly an order of magnitude; this efficiency is the under-appreciated edge public investors will rerate when these companies file.
insights.quote: a decent metric was sort of $300,000 of ARR per FTE. Now, that's the low end... we have several that are in the millions of dollars per FTE.

insights.claim: Richards argues 'speed is your moat' has replaced durability as the defining competitive trait of AI-era software: companies that don't innovate and ship at high product velocity get run over—not just by Anthropic and OpenAI but by other emerging players—unlike legacy SaaS whose moat was durability and low competition.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Reframes the durable-moat investing assumption: in AI-native software, velocity beats durability, so investors should weight product cadence over installed-base inertia.
insights.quote: the mantra among all these private companies is speed is your moat.

insights.claim: Richards flags a major under-discussed macro consequence: if SpaceX, Anthropic and OpenAI go public in 6-12 months, the liquidity flowing back to the LP ecosystem (endowments, nonprofits, universities) will be the highest ever after a 2020-2023 liquidity drought, re-fueling startup funding and innovation.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: The IPO wave's second-order effect is a liquidity flood into LPs that could reignite the entire VC/startup funding cycle—a positive feedback loop few are pricing.
insights.quote: the amount of liquidity that is going to come back into the LP ecosystem that have invested in venture capital and private equity will be the highest it's ever been

insights.claim: Richards notes public-market investors at last week's investment banking conference have zero exposure to SpaceX, Anthropic or OpenAI because they never participated in private rounds—so demand at IPO will hinge entirely on price, while private rounds (e.g., Databricks per Ali Ghodsi) have consistently been oversubscribed with far more demand than supply at $700-900B valuations.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Pent-up public demand exists but is price-sensitive; persistent oversubscription in private rounds (Databricks) at near-trillion valuations sets the anchor IPO investors must clear.
insights.quote: I was at an investment banking conference last week, and I was talking with a group of public investors, none of whom had exposure to any of these three

themes.theme: Late-cycle sentiment, Jensen's megaphone and froth signals

insights.claim: Nathan warns that Jensen Huang has become a sentiment problem: offhand comments drove a ~20% rally in ServiceNow and a ~30% rally in Marvell (after Nvidia's ~$2B investment and a 'next trillion-dollar semi' remark), and Nvidia's web of investments in Neoclouds, Intel and startups is increasingly circular and self-fulfilling—nothing has dented the company in 3.5-4 years, which itself is a warning.
insights.speaker: host (Dan Nathan)
insights.so_what: Nvidia's circular investments and Jensen's market-moving commentary are a froth/sentiment risk; the absence of any setback in 4 years is a late-cycle flag to watch.
insights.quote: Jensen Huang is becoming a real problem from a sentiment standpoint... he caused a 20% rally in ServiceNow... a 30% rally in Marvell

insights.claim: Richards offers two durable late-cycle maxims: 'nobody knows anything'—not Zuckerberg, Huang, Trump or anyone—about what happens tomorrow, and 'everybody talks their book,' so Jensen describing a bright future is simply his job as the CEO at the epicenter; he cites Elon Musk consistently missing 15 years of targets while still doing amazing things as the archetype.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: A discipline check: discount promotional CEO narratives ('everybody talks their book') when sizing AI-infra bets, especially around Nvidia and Tesla guidance.
insights.quote: nobody knows anything... And the other thing is everybody talks their book.

insights.claim: Both agree single-day 20-30% gaps in real companies (Intel, Dell, HPE up ~30%, Cisco, plus Snowflake and Datadog) are a genuine worry signal—distinct from meme stocks—forcing the binary read that either institutions are buying undervalued names or a retail rush is pushing stocks up unsustainably.
insights.speaker: host (Dan Nathan) and Jeff Richards
insights.so_what: Violent single-day melt-ups in legitimate large-cap tech are a late-cycle tell; the question of institutional vs. retail-driven moves is the key diagnostic.
insights.quote: when I see things go up 20 25 30% in a day does it cause me to worry yes for sure.

insights.claim: Richards cites Howard Lindzon's anecdote that Virgin Galactic (SPCE) jumped ~120% in days because retail investors mistakenly thought it might be SpaceX's ticker—calling that kind of mistaken-ticker buying the froth that genuinely worries him, alongside prediction markets and proposed 24/7 stock trading as new volatility sources.
insights.speaker: Jeff Richards, Notable Capital partner
insights.so_what: Mistaken-ticker manias (SPCE/SpaceX), prediction markets and 24/7 trading are structural volatility risks—classic 1999-style retail froth signals to monitor.
insights.quote: SPCE, which is Virgin Galactic, had gone up 120%... because people thought that might be the stock symbol for SpaceX.
